kRvIA5qoxCw.title: kRvIA5qoxCw
kRvIA5qoxCw.transcript: All right, I'd share this in written form on the X platform the other day and it was quite well received. Got over a quarter of a million views and so I thought I'd share this story of what was the most stressful moment or moments of my entire life. This is back in 2017. Just turned 40 years of age. I said I was planning on spending my 40th birthday and my wife at the time had arranged kind of an out-of-town ski trip. Had a bunch of friends who were supposed to be coming in, but instead so I was active duty military. I got sent to Afghanistan as a trauma surgeon. I was an orthopedic surgeon. I was there to take care of all the orthopedic trauma that was going on in a place called Bagram Airbase in in the middle of Afghanistan there, not far from the capital. About 6 weeks in this is now February. So I got there in early January just before my birthday. I turned 40 and I remember sitting in the dining facility eating a piece of cake kind of by myself celebrating my 40th birthday in a very different manner than I thought I I otherwise would have. So anyway, 6 weeks in by this time we'd been accustomed to all the trauma, you know, I I was relatively new in my orthopedic career. Remember I had started a little later as I had a period of time where I was a professional athlete before I went, you know, finished up my medical training. So I was a little bit older. I was 40. Just been out of my residency for a couple of years. You know, in the residency we we were exposed to, you know, a decent amount of trauma. We had things like train wrecks and refinery explosions and so on and so forth, but nothing anywhere near the level of devastation, destruction, and nonstop just onslaught of massive trauma on a daily basis like we saw in the middle of a war zone. And this 2007, this was a pretty busy time. You know, this was a lot of stuff going on every single day. Myself and my my partner Dr. Tom Large, another orthopedic surgeon who was also fresh out of training, were in charge of basically the biggest referral center in the entire war theater. So we got all the trauma that came in fresh locally plus all the stuff that we shipped in that was initially stabilized at what we call the A-5 or forward operating base and then brought to us. So we were, you know, always inundated with trauma. From time to time we would have dignitaries come by and and visit us to take, you know, photo ops. So we had like Nancy Pelosi came and various other members of Congress would come by to take their their pictures and say they support the troops and so on and so forth. Anyway, you know, day-to-day we're seeing lots and lots of trauma. Unfortunately, little kids being blown up by landmines. Some of you guys may remember that the Russians had tried to take over Afghanistan in the '80s and they left gazillions of landmines everywhere. So little kids always stepping on landmines and honestly much of what we took care of were non-combatants. I mean, these were civilians that we would take care of. We took care of a lot of Afghan army members. We took care of NATO forces. We took care of US forces. We even took care of enemy combatants. You know, our role there as surgeons and you just did the best you could to try to save as many lives as possible. So this one February at that time Vice President Dick Cheney is coming by for a visit and at the time the Taliban was going to try to make make it, you know, they found out about it and the word was they were going to try to assassinate him somehow. Well, the base was, you know, huge. I mean, it was a huge facility. Everybody was Everybody was armed. I mean, as a surgeon I had to walk around with a 9 mm strapped to my waist at all times basically. You know, we had the thought was a show of force would deter anyone from trying to overrun and take the base. And so there were, you know, tens of thousands of people walking around all all armed. Wasn't likely they were going to get him, but at our gate at our main gate often a lot of the locals would hang out. Little kids would be playing, you know, playing soccer. You know, there was there was a lot of a crowd around our gate because the reason was is because we always used to give away stuff. We'd give away clothing and food and other necessities to people as an active, you know, sort of humanitarianism I suppose. So this is a typical day. We were doing our typical trauma stuff, you know, in the OR, probably washing out some wounds or something, you know, every day we were busy just just dealing with trauma, doing initial surgeries, doing secondary surgeries, on and on and on, you know, always busy. And suddenly we are summoned that there's this massive casualty event occurred. So what had happened was while Dick Cheney was visiting a suicide bomber approached the gate and detonated himself and, you know, blew himself up and killed like 20-25 people instantly. There were another 25 or 30, you know, pretty critically wounded casualties at the same time. They would often in their suicide vests would pack them with things like ball bearings and nails and screws and other sharp objects and they would often cover them in feces. And the reason they would do that is because when those screws and nails penetrate people's flesh, if they were covered in feces the infection rates would go up quite a bit higher. If they didn't die from the initial blast, they would die subsequent to massive infection later. So it was always a a challenge dealing with blast wounds cuz they were always particularly dirty and then when covered in filth like that was even worse. So anyway, we go to the emergency room to to to do the initial triage. The initial triage looks something like this. You you kind of divide your casualties up into three groups. One group were the people that they would get a black tag. They're not going to make it. They're critically injured. There's no way you can save maybe they have exposed brain or their injuries are so massive there's no way that that you could save them. So those people were were kind of just basically, you know, kind of given medication to help them, you know, maybe pass more comfortably. The next group were the people that were injured, but wasn't critical. And maybe they had broken bones. Maybe they had, you know, extremity wounds. Maybe some, you know, kind of lacerations, but you could kind of stabilize those folks where they were and they didn't need to go in the operating room. And that's what I see. Those are the type of people as orthopedic surgeons we end up taking care of a lot of extremity trauma. But initially you have this third group, the people that were considered critically injured, but salvageable. And these people would often have internal bleeding like in their thoracic cavity or in their abdominal cavity. And so what we would do and as an orthopedic surgeon that's not really my my main area of purview, but we certainly can help out. And so the general surgeons, the belly surgeons were taking all these critically injured people back to our operating room. Now this hospital we were in was basically it was like a wooden shack with tin roofs and we had, you know, two conex boxes. You know, those things they put on trains to transport goods. That was our operating room. And I remember they were pretty low and the lights would hang down. I was always kind of ducking my head so I didn't whack into the lights cuz it was I considered sterile. And so we're operating these little tiny rooms there taking these people back there to try to save their lives. So we're in there and I can remember, you know, we're about an hour into this and we're in there on a case this local Afghan civilian is, you know, bleeding to death and we're we're just, you know, frantically trying to find bleeders inside his belly. So we're digging through intestines, you know, we're cauterizing small bleeders and ligating some of the bigger vessels that might have been injured looking for anything that any sources of bleeding. And one thing that was interesting is that the Taliban often would would have a bomb and then what they would do is often, you know, wait for the first responders and they would often like sneak another person in pretending to be a casualty who would also had a bomb. And so when the first responders got there then they would blow that one up and and kill all the first responders. And that was a well-known sort of practice that they would do. So we're in the middle of the OR. There's about five of us. I remember a guy named Jeff Parker who was the general surgeon was kind of leading the case. I was in there assisting. Anesthesia, there I think there was another surgeon scrubbed in, scrub techs. There was about five of us in this conex box. And you know, just as that's going on, we get a nurse comes in the room and says, "Hey, we think there is another bomber within the hospital." And dressed up as a casualty. So we and we knew this happened. And so we're sitting there and, you know, we're like I said, the hospital's tiny. I mean, it's not maybe 100 ft in the whole in total length. And so at that moment I I remember Jeff looks up and he says, "Hey guys, do you want to keep going and try to save this guy or do we get the hell out of here?" And I remember, you know, I remember my butt cheeks clenched up a little bit cuz it was pretty nerve-racking. And I remember really without hesitation all of us said, "Fuck it. Let's just keep going." All right. So we're in there head down and for the next, you know, 15-20 minutes we're just, you know, operating as fast as we can. So we get and waiting, you know, are we going to get Is a bomb going to go off cuz we don't know, right? You know, in the hospital they're evacuating the hospital. They're sending people sweep in for for bombs. You know, I'm in there operating away and we finish the case. We we, you know, we find the bleeders. We pack the wound with with with gauze. Throw throw a sterile dressing on there and then get the hell out of there. We wheel them out to the to the the evacuation zone which is, you know, a couple hundred feet away from the building. And we're standing there and they find out there was not a bomber in fact. But for for, you know, 15-20 minutes, you know, we're in there not knowing we're going to die or not. So that was pretty stressful as you might imagine. Now, what I am happy to say is I was pretty proud of the selflessness that everybody in that room demonstrated. In fact, the whole medical team, you know, dealing with this stuff. And this is something, you know, you don't often hear these stories from war, but it's it's really such a stressful environment and it's good to see people coming together. You know, I'm not a fan of war by the way and, you know, I I I wish we could avoid as much as possible. But this is again for me one of the most profound memories of my life and it is, you know, something that will always, you know, shape me and and and the way I way I think about things, the way I prioritize things. And I, you know, like I said, it's always try to do the right thing. Anyway, that's my story and hope you guys enjoyed it.

pACa2jX9B7Y.transcript: Why do we even care about diabetes? Well, we care about diabetes not necessarily because blood sugar is just regulated, although it does have an impact. We really care about the long-term outcomes, heart disease, kidney failure, blindness, amputations, and so on and so forth. And one of the things that um is important to realize is that a lot of complications from diabetes can be avoided with proper lifestyle and nutrition. And so this new study out of Japan, which was published in the Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism, shows that Japanese folks that are diabetic that consume high carbohydrate diets, particularly in the setting of low animal products, so kind of a vegan-esque plant-based high-carb diet more or less, are more likely to develop heart disease. Now, um this is not shocking to me at all, but some of you guys might find that to be interesting because we always told the diabetics we should avoid fat and avoid animal products. This is what the sort of the common sort of prescriptions might be. Now, again, this is an epidemiologic observational study, so the the usual limitations are need to be applied here. This is hypothesis generating. It does not The data that is obtained from this does not show cause or anything like that. Now, how would we do that? Well, there are some interesting studies out there. Certainly there are a lot of low-carb diets that show pretty good favorable results when it comes to diabetes with regard to glucose control, hemoglobin A1C, and to some degree levels of you know, end organ damage. Now, there is a research by the name of Andrew Koutnik who I've interviewed, really nice guy, about a month ago, and he did a longitudinal case report on himself. Now, he is a type 1 diabetic. He treats that with a low-carb diet, plenty of animal foods in there, and he's had excellent outcomes. He did a very high-level vascular examination looking at cardiovascular disease. In fact, the longest period ever tested in any humans was done by him in this particular instance. And what he found was that his vascular health was excellent for a diabetic. In fact, it was excellent for any um you know, non-diabetic. So, he's showing that with appropriate nutrition and lifestyle, he works out as well, um you can basically prevent or at least delay the the onset of diabetic-related complications probably for a long time, probably to the point where you're less at less risk than someone who doesn't have diabetes. And so, um you know, where should we go with this? Obviously, we should do more studies on this. Um I I do know that there is a diabetes intervention trial using a carnivore diet that is in the works right now. I'm in the behind-the-scenes helping to get that thing organized, so I'm excited to see that come out. Probably out about a year from now. Anyway, new study, interesting hypothesis. I think we need to get away from the old message that animal fat is killing us and making us diabetic and making things worse because that does not seem to be the case. And you know, we couple that with the thousands and thousands and thousands of anecdotes of those people on carnivore diets that have diabetes that see their diabetes get better, and not just the fact that their blood glucose is more stable, but the fact that I've seen people saying that their retinopathy in their eyes has reversing, their kidney disease is reversing. So, this is important stuff, guys. Let me know what you think in the comments. I'll take care.
